title: Sony erases digital content from libraries; we're reminded we don’t own what we buy
gist: Sony is removing 551 previously purchased StudioCanal titles from UK PlayStation Store libraries starting September 1 due to lapsing licensing agreements, the latest in a recurring pattern showing that digital media 'purchases' are really revocable long-term licenses.

themes.theme: Digital ownership is an illusion by design

insights.claim: Digital rentals and purchases from platforms like the PlayStation Store are legally long-term licenses valid only as long as the platform retains distribution rights, not true ownership, and that license term is often finite.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Investors and consumers evaluating digital media/content platforms should model licensing-dependency risk into any 'owned library' value proposition; this is a structural feature, not a one-off failure, across the industry.
insights.quote: we don’t own the stuff we purchase digitally
insights.locator: paragraph beginning 'Regardless, the incident is a reminder'

insights.claim: Consumer and critic frustration centers not just on losing access but on platforms using the word 'purchase' for what is functionally a revocable long-term rental, suggesting potential consumer-protection/labeling exposure for digital storefronts.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Mislabeling risk could eventually invite regulatory or class-action scrutiny of digital storefronts' marketing language, a tail risk for platforms like PlayStation Store, Amazon, Apple, and Google that use 'buy' language for licensed content.
insights.quote: Sony's announcement has frustrated some, including those who believe Sony should offer refunds or who think digital stores should stop using terms like “purchase” for long-term rentals.

themes.theme: Sony's track record of pulling purchased content

insights.claim: Sony will remove 551 StudioCanal titles (including Paddington, Terminator 2: Judgment Day, and Rambo 3) from UK PlayStation Store customers' libraries starting September 1, 2026, due to expiring content licensing agreements.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term date (September 1, 2026) to watch for whether Sony renegotiates with StudioCanal in time, as it previously did with Discovery in 2023 — a template for predicting resolution likelihood.
insights.quote: As of September 1, affected customers will no longer be able to stream 551 titles from the PlayStation Store.

insights.claim: Sony has a repeated pattern of pulling purchased digital content: 1,318 Discovery show seasons threatened for removal in 2023 (later reversed after relicensing), 314 StudioCanal titles actually pulled in Germany and Austria in 2022, and full deletion of users' Funimation libraries after merging it into Crunchyroll.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A multi-year, multi-territory pattern (2022, 2023, 2026) indicates this is systemic behavior rather than an isolated incident, raising the base-rate probability that current PlayStation Store libraries elsewhere will face similar cuts.

insights.claim: Sony stopped selling movie and TV show rentals and purchases entirely in August 2021, signaling a broader multi-year retreat from operating a digital video storefront.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: This strategic retreat suggests Sony sees declining value in maintaining a digital video storefront business, implying it has less incentive to preserve legacy licensing deals for previously sold titles going forward.
insights.quote: Sony has also been scaling down its digital store and stopped selling movie and show rentals and purchases in August 2021.
insights.locator: paragraph on Sony scaling down digital store

insights.claim: Sony previously reversed a planned removal of Discovery content in 2023 within a few weeks of announcing it, after updating its licensing arrangement with Discovery, showing removal announcements are sometimes negotiating leverage rather than final outcomes.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Precedent suggests a possible reprieve for StudioCanal titles is plausible before September 1, but the author frames this as decreasingly likely given Sony's broader pullback from digital video.
